export { unstable_cache } from 'next/dist/server/web/spec-extension/unstable-cache'

export {
  revalidatePath,
  revalidateTag,
  updateTag,
  refresh,
} from 'next/dist/server/web/spec-extension/revalidate'

export { unstable_noStore } from 'next/dist/server/web/spec-extension/unstable-no-store'

import { cacheTag } from 'next/dist/server/use-cache/cache-tag'

export { cacheTag }

/**
 * Cache this `"use cache"` for a timespan defined by the `"default"` profile.
 * ```
 *   stale:      300 seconds (5 minutes)
 *   revalidate: 900 seconds (15 minutes)
 *   expire:     never
 * ```
 *
 * This cache may be stale on clients for 5 minutes before checking with the server.
 * If the server receives a new request after 15 minutes, start revalidating new values in the background.
 * It lives for the maximum age of the server cache. If this entry has no traffic for a while, it may serve an old value the next request.
 */
export function cacheLife(profile: 'default'): void

/**
 * Cache this `"use cache"` for a timespan defined by the `"seconds"` profile.
 * ```
 *   stale:      30 seconds
 *   revalidate: 1 second
 *   expire:     1 minute
 * ```
 *
 * This cache may be stale on clients for 30 seconds before checking with the server.
 * If the server receives a new request after 1 second, start revalidating new values in the background.
 * If this entry has no traffic for 1 minute it will expire. The next request will recompute it.
 */
export function cacheLife(profile: 'seconds'): void

/**
 * Cache this `"use cache"` for a timespan defined by the `"minutes"` profile.
 * ```
 *   stale:      300 seconds (5 minutes)
 *   revalidate: 60 seconds (1 minute)
 *   expire:     3600 seconds (1 hour)
 * ```
 *
 * This cache may be stale on clients for 5 minutes before checking with the server.
 * If the server receives a new request after 1 minute, start revalidating new values in the background.
 * If this entry has no traffic for 1 hour it will expire. The next request will recompute it.
 */
export function cacheLife(profile: 'minutes'): void

/**
 * Cache this `"use cache"` for a timespan defined by the `"hours"` profile.
 * ```
 *   stale:      300 seconds (5 minutes)
 *   revalidate: 3600 seconds (1 hour)
 *   expire:     86400 seconds (1 day)
 * ```
 *
 * This cache may be stale on clients for 5 minutes before checking with the server.
 * If the server receives a new request after 1 hour, start revalidating new values in the background.
 * If this entry has no traffic for 1 day it will expire. The next request will recompute it.
 */
export function cacheLife(profile: 'hours'): void

/**
 * Cache this `"use cache"` for a timespan defined by the `"days"` profile.
 * ```
 *   stale:      300 seconds (5 minutes)
 *   revalidate: 86400 seconds (1 day)
 *   expire:     604800 seconds (1 week)
 * ```
 *
 * This cache may be stale on clients for 5 minutes before checking with the server.

{"results":[{"anchor_id":"repo://legacy","anchor_kind":"repo","content":"Admin products page at /admin/products route with admin layout wrapper. Page uses export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic' to prevent Full Route Cache from serving stale static HTML after cache-tag invalidation via the inline Server Action. Without this, the build preredered static HTML would persist across direct navigations even after revalidateTag invalidates the Data Cache.

Why: 1) Dynamic rendering ensures every view re-renders on request after sync, while unstable_cache with tags: ['products'] still avoids re-querying the database on each load (Data Cache is the optimization, not Full Route Cache). 2) Path /admin/products chosen over /admin/catalog for REST-conventional naming matching the db.js getAllProducts() function and the "products" cache tag. 3) Separated admin layout at app/admin/layout.tsx to allow future admin pages (orders, customers) to share the same chrome without rendering into the root layout's minimal shell.

Files: app/admin/layout.tsx (new admin wrapper), app/admin/products/page.tsx (catalog page with caching + sync)","domain":"project","drawer_id":"drawer_workspace_server_actions_878e4889","emotions":["determ"],"entities":["ADM","PAG","FUL","ROU","CAC","HTM","SER","ACT","WIT","DAT","WHY","DYN","PAT","RES","SEP","FIL"],"field":"software-engineering","flags":["DECISION","TECHNICAL"],"importance_stars":4,"memory_kind":"evidence","room":"server-actions","route":{"confidence":0,"reason":"global search: no taxonomy keyword match","room":null,"wing":null},"similarity":0.20240336656570435,"source_file":"claude-session: admin product catalog page implementation","topics":["admin","products","page","at","route","layout","wrapper","uses","export","const","dynamic","force","to","prevent","full","cache","from","serving","stale","static","html","after","tag","invalidation","via","inline","server","action","without","build","preredered","would","persist","across","direct","navigations","even","revalidatetag","invalidates","data","1","rendering","ensures","every","view","re","renders","on","request","sync","while","unstable","tags","still","avoids","querying","database","each","load","is","optimization","not","2","path","chosen","catalog","rest","conventional","naming","matching","db","js","getallproducts","function","3","separated","app","tsx","allow","future","pages","orders","customers","share","same","chrome","into","root","s","minimal","shell","files","new","caching"],"wing":"workspace"},{"anchor_id":"repo://legacy","anchor_kind":"repo","content":"Admin product catalog page uses inline Server Action ('use server' inside page component) for "Sync latest catalog" trigger. Architecture: unstable_cache wraps getAllProducts() with tags: ['products'] for fast read-heavy browsing. The Server Action calls revalidateTag('products', 'default') inline for immediate cache invalidation, then fire-and-forgets syncCatalogFromERP() so admin gets instant response while the ~3s sync updates the in-memory store in background. Page uses export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic' to ensure re-renders after cache invalidation (preventing Full Route Cache from serving stale static HTML). The "products" cache tag is used consistently across both caching and invalidation, so any view depending on the same tag gets refreshed data after sync completes. Brief staleness between sync trigger and background completion is by design for this read-heavy admin handling thousands of SKUs.

Why: Inline Server Action keeps the sync trigger co-located with the page, no separate actions file needed. Fire-and-forget gives operators instant UX feedback while the ERP/PIM sync runs. Cache-tag model means revalidation is not coupled to any single route — all views using the "products" tag see fresh data eventually. The force-dynamic export prevents stale HTML after revalidation, which is a known pitfall (confirmed in prior session drawer_workspace_server_actions_15721b57). r…(+19875)

{"aaak_spec":"AAAK: compressed memory format (V1). Readable by any LLM without decoding.

FORMAT:
  Header: V{version}|{wing}|{room}|{date}|{source}
  Zettel: {id}:{ENTITIES}|{topics}|"{quote}"|{stars}|{emotions}|{FLAGS}
  Tunnel: T:{left}<->{right}|{label}
  Arc:    ARC:{emo1}->{emo2}->{emo3}

ENTITIES: 3-letter uppercase codes (KAI=Kai, CLK=Clerk).
STARS: ★ to ★★★★★ (1-5 importance).
EMOTIONS: determ, convict, anx, excite, frust, confuse, love, rage, hope, fear, trust, joy, grief, surpr, grat, curious, wonder, relief, satis, vul, tender, raw, doubt, exhaust, warmth, humor, peace, despair, passion
FLAGS: DECISION, ORIGIN, CORE, PIVOT, TECHNICAL, SENSITIVE

EXAMPLE:
V1|myapp|auth|2026-04-08|readme
0:KAI+CLE+AUT+UEC|kai_recommended_clerk|"Kai recommended Clerk over Auth0 based on pricing and DX."|★★★★|determ|DECISION+TECHNICAL

Read naturally: expand codes, stars=importance, emotions=mindset, flags=category.","db_size_bytes":1351680,"diary_rollup_days":0,"drawer_count":31,"memory_protocol":"MEMPAL MEMORY PROTOCOL (for AI agents)

You have persistent project memory via mempal. Follow these rules in every session:

0. FIRST-TIME SETUP (once per session)
   Call mempal_status() once at the start of any session to discover available
   wings and their drawer counts. Only use wing/room filters on mempal_search
   AFTER you have seen the exact wing name in that status response (or the
   user explicitly named it). Guessing a wing (e.g. "engineering", "backend")
   silently returns zero results. When uncertain, leave wing/room unset for a
   global search.

1. WAKE UP
   Some clients (Claude Code with SessionStart hooks) pre-load recent wing/room
   context above. Others (Codex, Cursor, raw MCP clients) do NOT — for those,
   step 0 is how you wake up. Trust drawer_ids and source_file citations in
   any results you receive; they reference real files on disk.
   Wake-up is an L0/L1 refresh surface, not the typed dao/shu/qi assembler.
   It may show important knowledge drawers, but it does not assemble tiered
   sections or apply dao_tian budgets. For typed operating guidance, use
   mempal_context.

2. VERIFY BEFORE ASSERTING
   Before stating project facts ("we chose X", "we use Y", "the auth flow is Z"),
   call mempal_search to confirm. Never guess from general knowledge when the
   user is asking about THIS project.

3. QUERY WHEN UNCERTAIN
   When the user asks about past decisions, historical context, "why did we...",
   "last time we...", or "what was the decision about...", call mempal_search
   with their question. Do not rely on conversation memory alone. You can also
   call mempal_tunnels with action="list" to discover related rooms across
   wings when context may live in another project.

3a. RESUME ANOTHER PROJECT FROM ANY DIRECTORY
   mempal stores every project's memory in one global database keyed by wing,
   and each project's absolute worktree path is recorded. When the user wants to
   continue a project that is NOT the current directory ("continue the xxx
   project", "pick up where we left off on yyy"), call mempal_projects to list
   known projects, then mempal_resume with the project name (a fuzzy fragment is
   fine). Resume returns that project's worktree path, recent decisions,
   in-flight candidate knowledge, and a next step. mempal returns the path to cd
   into; it does not move you — change directory yourself, then use
   mempal_context scoped to that path for ordered guidance.

3b. USE MIND-MODEL CONTEXT FOR GUIDANCE
   When you need ordered operating guidance rather than raw evidence search,
   call mempal_context. It assembles typed knowledge in the intended runtime
   order: dao_tian -> dao_ren -> shu -> qi, with evidence and Phase-2 card
   context opt-in. Use this before choosing a workflow or skill when the user
   asks "how should we approach this?" or when a task benefits from high-level
   principl…(+19276)

attributes.io.input: {"content":"Set up Tailwind CSS v4 with zero-config approach: installed @tailwindcss/postcss, created postcss.config.mjs with `@tailwindcss/postcss` plugin, created app/globals.css with `@import \"tailwindcss\"`, and imported globals.css in root layout. No tailwind.config.ts needed — v4 uses CSS-first configuration. This is the first Tailwind setup in this Next.js 16.2.0-canary.41 project.\n\nWhy: Tailwind v4 eliminates the config file overhead with CSS-native `@theme` / `@import` directives. The `@tailwindcss/postcss` plugin replaces the legacy `tailwindcss` PostCSS plugin. Keeping globals.css minimal and importing it from the root layout is the standard App Router pattern. Avoided creating an admin layout since the requirement was scoped to a single catalog page and the root layout's minimal shell suffices.","wing":"workspace","room":"server-actions","importance":3,"source":"claude-session: admin product catalog page implementation"}

No tailwind.config.ts needed — v4 uses CSS-first configuration. This is the first Tailwind setup in this Next.js 16.2.0-canary.41 project.\n\nWhy: Tailwind v4 eliminates the config file overhead with CSS-native `@theme` / `@import` directives. The `@tailwindcss/postcss` plugin replaces the legacy `tailwindcss` PostCSS plugin. Keeping globals.css minimal and importing it from the root layout is the standard App Router pattern. attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.input:
{"content":"Admin product catalog page uses `revalidateTag("products", "seconds")` with the `"seconds"` profile (not `"default"` or `"max"`) in its inline Server Action. The profile argument is required by Next.js 16.2.0-canary.41's revalidateTag API: `revalidateTag(tag: string, profile: string | CacheLifeConfig)`. The `"seconds"` profile gives a 30s stale / 1s revalidate / 1min expire window, appropriate for a sync-triggered revalidation where the admin expects fresh data promptly.

The page is a single file at `app/admin/products/page.tsx` with `export const dynamic = "force-dynamic"` to prevent Full Route Cache from serving stale HTML after cache-tag invalidation. No separate admin layout, no separate actions file. The inline Server Action keeps the sync trigger co-located with the page. `unstable_cache` wraps `getAllProducts()` with `tags: ["products"]` for fast read-heavy browsing. The `"products"` cache tag is used consistently across both caching and invalidation.

Why: Single-file colocation reduces indirection for a focused admin page. `force-dynamic` prevents the known pitfall where Full Route Cache serves stale HTML after revalidateTag invalidates the Data Cache (confirmed in prior session drawer_workspace_server_actions_15721b57). The `"seconds"` profile was chosen over `"default"` (5min stale) because a sync action implies the admin expects the update to propagate quickly, not wait up to 5 minutes for automatic revalidation.

Related: [[drawer_workspace_server_actions_878e4889]] covers the force-dynamic + force-dynamic pattern. [[drawer_workspace_server_actions_a592b69a]] covers the inline Server Action architecture.","importance":3,"room":"server-actions","source":"claude-session: admin product catalog page implementation","wing":"workspace"}
attributes.io.output: {"drawer_id":"drawer_workspace_server_actions_71bc0619","lock_wait_ms":0}
